content.full_text:
-_ ......... fOT1l.ia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance! Administration 
Executive!legal 
Enforcement 
Conflict of I nteres! 
(916) 322·5660 
Honorable Richard Robinson 
The State Assembly 
State Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
322·5901 
322·6441 
October 5, 1977 
Re: 
Opinion Request 
No. 77-027 
Dear Mr. Robinson: 
Thank you for your letter to Daniel Lowenstein of 
August 30, 1977, requesting an opinion from the Commission. 
Because your question has been addressed in two previous 
opinions issued by the Commission, no formal opinion will be 
issued in this case. 
However, I hope the following informal 
comments answer your question. 
You pose the following question: 
Is properly reported reimbursement for 
staff, constituent entertainment and 
political travel by an office holder, 
and reported by a campaign committee, 
reportable again as income on the Con-
flict of Interest statement? 
322·6444 
The Commission has held that expenses paid by an officeholder's 
campaign committee are not reportable on the officeholder's 
personal Statement of Economic Interests if the expenses 
relate to the official's political or governmental activities. 
See opinions requested by Janet Gray Hayes, 1 FPPC Opinions 
210 (75-145, Dec. 4, 1975) and Willie L. Brown, Jr, 1 FPPC 
Opinions 67 (75-055, July 2, 1975) (copies of these opinions 
are enclosed). 
The expenses you describe appear to fit that 
description. 
An officeholder is obligated to disclose a 
payment which his campaign committee makes as a gift on his 
Statement of Economic Interests only if the payment provides 


Honorable Richard Robinson 
October 5, 1977 
Page Two 
a purely personal benefit to the officeholder, such as the 
purchase of furniture for his home. 
Otherwise, the payment 
need only be disclosed on the committee's campaign statement. 
I trust this answers your question. 
If you wish to 
appeal the denial of your opinion request, you may do so 
pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. 
If you have any 
questions, please contact Michael Baker, Director of the 
Commission's Legal Division. 
MB:MJB:plh 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Michael Bennett 
Executive Director

Enclosures
sections.conclusion: The Commission has held that expenses paid by an officeholder's campaign committee are not reportable on the officeholder's personal Statement of Economic Interests if the expenses relate to the official's political or governmental activities. An officeholder is obligated to disclose a payment which his campaign committee makes as a gift on his Statement of Economic Interests only if the payment provides a purely personal benefit to the officeholder, such as the purchase of furniture for his home. Otherwise, the payment need only be disclosed on the committee's campaign statement.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether campaign committee reimbursements for staff, constituent entertainment, and political travel that were already reported must be reported again as income on a personal Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign committee expenses for political or governmental activities are not reportable on an officeholder's personal conflict of interest statement; they need only be disclosed on the campaign committee's statement. Only payments providing purely personal benefits must be disclosed on the Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Informal opinion letter declining to issue formal opinion (Opinion Request No. 77-027); references two prior FPPC opinions (75-145 and 75-055); clear question and conclusion sections present; OCR quality appears good with minor formatting artifacts.

CONCLUSION: The Commission has held that expenses paid by an officeholder's campaign committee are not reportable on the officeholder's personal Statement of Economic Interests if the expenses relate to the official's political or governmental activities. An officeholder is obligated to disclose a payment which his campaign committee makes as a gift on his Statement of Economic Interests only if the payment provides a purely personal benefit to the officeholder, such as the purchase of furniture for his home. Otherwise, the payment need only be disclosed on the committee's campaign statement.

Because your question has been addressed in two previous opinions issued by the Commission, no formal opinion will be issued in this case. However, I hope the following informal comments answer your question. You pose the following question: Is properly reported reimbursement for staff, constituent entertainment and political travel by an office holder, and reported by a campaign committee, reportable again as income on the Con- flict of Interest statement? 322·6444 The Commission has held that expenses paid by an officeholder's campaign committee are not reportable on the officeholder's personal Statement of Economic Interests if the expenses relate to the official's political or governmental activities. See opinions requested by Janet Gray Hayes, 1 FPPC Opinions 210 (75-145, Dec. 4, 1975) and Willie L. Brown, Jr, 1 FPPC Opinions 67 (75-055, July 2, 1975) (copies of these opinions are enclosed). The expenses you describe appear to fit that description. An officeholder is obligated to disclose a payment which his campaign committee makes as a gift on his Statement of Economic Interests only if the payment provides Honorable Richard Robinson October 5, 1977 Page Two a purely personal benefit to the officeholder, such as the purchase of furniture for his home. Otherwise, the payment need only be disclosed on the committee's campaign statement. I trust this answers your question. embedding.summary: FPPC declines to issue a formal opinion to Assemblyman Richard Robinson regarding whether campaign committee reimbursements for political expenses require reporting on a personal conflict of interest statement, citing two prior opinions holding that such expenses relating to political or governmental activities need only be reported on the campaign statement.
